content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 26, 1990

Carolyn Richardson
California Farm Bureau Federation
1601 Exposition Blvd.
Sacramento, CA 95815

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-431

Dear Ms. Richardson:

You have requested advice on behalf of the California Farm
Bureau Federation ("Farm Bureau") concerning the lobbying
provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹ By telephone on July 11,
1990, you indicated that you have also been authorized to request
this advice on behalf of the Western Growers' Association ("WGA").

QUESTION

What reporting requirements would the Farm Bureau, a lobbyist
employer, its employee-lobbyist, or Western Growers' Association,
another lobbyist employer, incur if the Farm Bureau's lobbyist
provides free lobbying services to WGA?

CONCLUSION

So long as the lobbyist is not being paid by the Farm Bureau
for his or her time lobbying for WGA, and WGA pays only for
reasonable travel expenses incurred by the lobbyist while lobbying
on its behalf, neither the Farm Bureau nor the lobbyist have any
additional reporting obligations.

WGA must report on its lobbyist employer report those
legislative or administrative actions the Farm Bureau lobbyist
attempted to influence on its behalf. In addition, all reasonable
travel paid by WGA on behalf of the Farm Bureau lobbyist must be
reported in Part III (Other Payments) of its lobbyist employer
report, Form 635.

___________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Carolyn Richardson
Page Two


FACTS

An in-house employee-lobbyist for the Farm Bureau intends to
lobby on behalf of WGA, another lobbyist employer. The lobbyist
will not be compensated by either the Farm Bureau or WGA for
attempting to influence legislative or administrative action on
behalf of WGA. WGA will pay for reasonable travel expenses
incurred by the lobbyist in connection with attempting to
influence legislative or administrative action its behalf.

ANALYSIS

A person who employs a lobbyist for economic consideration,
other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, for the
purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action is
considered a "lobbyist employer." (Section 82039.5.)

Section 86116 requires lobbyist employers to report, in part:

...the total amount of all payments to lobbyists employed by
the employer, ...a description of the lobbying interests of
the employer, (and) ...the total of all other payments to
influence legislative or administrative action including
overhead expenses...

The actions of the Farm Bureau lobbyist on behalf of WGA do
not affect the reporting requirements of the Farm Bureau as a
lobbyist employer.

A lobbyist is someone who:

...is employed or contracts for economic consideration, other
than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, to
communicate directly or through his or her agents with any
elective state official, agency official or legislative
official for the purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action...

(Section 82039.)

Normally, when a lobbyist lobbies on behalf of two different
entities, the lobbyist must register as a lobbying firm, even if
the lobbyist is an employee of one of the entities. (Keese Advice
Letter, No. A-86-124, copy enclosed.) However, since the Farm
Bureau's lobbyist will not be receiving compensation from WGA, the
Farm Bureau lobbyist does not qualify as a lobbyist for WGA and,
therefore, is not required to register as a lobbying firm.
(Regulation 18238.5, copy enclosed.)

Carolyn Richardson
Page Three

Section 86113, which establishes the reporting requirements
for lobbyists, does not require the lobbyist to report payments
received nor the legislative or administrative actions the
lobbyist attempted to influence. Therefore, the Farm Bureau
lobbyist does not incur additional reporting requirements by
providing services to WGA gratis.

In addition, because WGA is paying only reasonable travel
expenses for the Farm Bureau lobbyist, WGA is not required to
amend its registration to add the Farm Bureau lobbyist as one
employed to lobby on its behalf. This is consistent with the
exception in Sections 82039 and 82039.5 that such payments alone
are not counted for purposes of qualifying an individual as a
lobbyist or an entity as a lobbyist employer.

However, since Section 86116 requires lobbyist employers to
report "the total of all other payments to influence legislative
or administrative actions," WGA must report any payments it makes
to support the Farm Bureau's lobbyist's activities made on its
behalf, including reasonable travel expenses. Such payments
should be included on WGA's Form 635, Part III, Section D, Other
Payments to Influence. In addition, WGA is required to report the
legislative or administrative actions the Farm Bureau lobbyist
will attempt to influence on its behalf.

If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabyln
Acting General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Braaten-Moen
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

WGA will pay for reasonable travel expenses incurred by the lobbyist in connection with attempting to influence legislative or administrative action its behalf.
sections.analysis:
A person who employs a lobbyist for economic consideration, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action is considered a "lobbyist employer." (Section 82039.5.)

Section 86116 requires lobbyist employers to report, in part:

...the total amount of all payments to lobbyists employed by the employer, ...a description of the lobbying interests of the employer, (and) ...the total of all other payments to influence legislative or administrative action including overhead expenses...

The actions of the Farm Bureau lobbyist on behalf of WGA do not affect the reporting requirements of the Farm Bureau as a lobbyist employer.

A lobbyist is someone who:

...is employed or contracts for economic consideration, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, to communicate directly or through his or her agents with any elective state official, agency official or legislative official for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action...

(Section 82039.)

Normally, when a lobbyist lobbies on behalf of two different entities, the lobbyist must register as a lobbying firm, even if the lobbyist is an employee of one of the entities. (Keese Advice Letter, No. A-86-124, copy enclosed.) However, since the Farm Bureau's lobbyist will not be receiving compensation from WGA, the Farm Bureau lobbyist does not qualify as a lobbyist for WGA and, therefore, is not required to register as a lobbying firm. (Regulation 18238.5, copy enclosed.)

Section 86113, which establishes the reporting requirements for lobbyists, does not require the lobbyist to report payments received nor the legislative or administrative actions the lobbyist attempted to influence. Therefore, the Farm Bureau lobbyist does not incur additional reporting requirements by providing services to WGA gratis.

In addition, because WGA is paying only reasonable travel expenses for the Farm Bureau lobbyist, WGA is not required to amend its registration to add the Farm Bureau lobbyist as one employed to lobby on its behalf. This is consistent with the exception in Sections 82039 and 82039.5 that such payments alone are not counted for purposes of qualifying an individual as a lobbyist or an entity as a lobbyist employer.

However, since Section 86116 requires lobbyist employers to report "the total of all other payments to influence legislative or administrative action," WGA must report any payments it makes to support the Farm Bureau's lobbyist's activities made on its behalf, including reasonable travel expenses. Such payments should be included on WGA's Form 635, Part III, Section D, Other Payments to Influence. In addition, WGA is required to report the legislative or administrative actions the Farm Bureau lobbyist will attempt to influence on its behalf.
